nuscale_ch05_0005.title: System Components
nuscale_ch05_0005.content:
NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report Summary Description
Tier 2 5.1-3 Revision 5opened to allow feedwater to recirculate through the DHRS heat exchangers and 
secondary water chemistry conditions for we t layup are established. The SGs are then 
isolated from the main steam and feedwater systems. PZR wate r level is reduced using the 
chemical volume control system to match th e water level in the CNV in preparation for 
opening the RVVs and RRVs. Nitrogen is vent ed from the PZR through the RPV high point 
degasification line until PZR pressure matc hes containment pressu re. When the PZR and 
containment pressure and water level are matched, the RVVs and RRVs are opened.
5.1.3 System Components
5.1.3.1 Reactor Pressure Vessel
The RPV is a metal vessel that forms part of the RCPB and is  a barrier to the release of 
fission products. Most of the reactor coolant is contained in the RPV. The RPV is 
supported laterally and vertically by th e CNV. The RPV contains and supports the 
reactor core, RVI, steam generators, and PZR.  The RPV provides pe netrations, support, 
and alignment for the control rod drive syst em. The RPV also provides penetrations and 
attachment locations for the RCS instruments, the ECCS valves (RVVs and RRVs), and 
RCS piping (RCS injection, RCS discharg e, PZR spray supply, and RPV high point 
degasification lines). In addition, the RPV also provides steam and feedwater plenums 
for the steam generators and RSV penetrations.
The RPV and appurtenances are fu rther described in Section 5.3. 
5.1.3.2 Reactor Cool ant System Piping
The RCS piping external to the RPV consists of the following lines: 
• two PZR spray line branches from a common spray header 
• RPV high point degasification line 
• RCS injection line 
• RCS discharge line 
The RCS injection line has branch lines that connect to each of the ECCS valve reset 
valves.
The RCS piping is further described in Section 5.4.
5.1.3.3 Pressurizer 
The PZR, which is integral to  the RPV, occupies the volume inside the RPV above the 
PZR baffle plate. The RCS co mponents in the PZR volume  are the PZR spray nozzles 
(which are part of the RVI) and the PZR heat er assemblies. The PZR provides a point in 
the RCS where liquid and vapor are main tained in equilibrium under saturated 
conditions at a temperature greater than THOT for pressure contro l of the RCS during 
steady-state operations and transients. Maintaining the saturated conditions higher 
than THOT ensures the reactor cool ant remains subcooled duri ng normal operations. 
The PZR also serves as a surge volume.NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report Summary Description
Tier 2 5.1-4 Revision 5The PZR is further described in Section 5.4.
5.1.3.4 Reactor Vessel Internals
The RVI contain several sub-assemblies th at provide support and alignment for the 
core, the control rod assemblies, the control rod drive shafts, survei llance capsules, and 
the instrument guide tubes. Additionally, the RVI channel reactor coolant flow from the reactor core to the SGs and back within the RPV.
The RVI sub-assemblies include the core support assembly, surveillance capsule 
assemblies, lower riser assembly, upper rise r assembly, flow diverter, and PZR spray 
nozzles.
The RVI are further described in  Section 3.9.5 and Section 5.3.
5.1.3.5 Reactor Safety Valves
Two pilot operated, self-actuating RSVs connect to the top of the RPV upper head and 
discharge directly to the CNV. These valv es are a part of the RCPB and provide 
overpressure protection as required by th e American Society of Mechanical Engineers 
(ASME) Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code. 
The RSVs are further described in Section 5.2.
5.1.3.6 Emergency Core Cooling Valves
The ECCS valves consist of three RVVs an d two RRVs. The RVVs connect to the upper 
head portion of the RPV and discharge the PZR steam space directly to the CNV. Two 
RRVs connect to the RPV shell just above the main closure flange. When opened, they permit recirculation of water in the CNV ba ck into the RPV and ultimately through the 
core. The ECCS valves are a part of the RCPB and are used during emergency core 
cooling operation. The RVVs also provide overpressure protection during operations at low temperature conditions.
The RVVs and RRVs are furthe r described in Section 6.3.
5.1.3.7 Steam Generators
The SGS is an integral part of the RP V (although not a component of the RCS) 
composed of the SG tubes, SG tube support s, steam and feedwater piping inside 
containment, SG tube inlet flow restrictor s, feed plenums, and steam plenums. The 
design contains two independent, but inte rtwined, SGs located inside the RPV that 
facilitate heat transfer to the secondary co olant system and provide redundancy for the 
DHRS. The SGs are a once-through, helical-c oil design, with primary-side reactor 
coolant outside the tubes and secondary-side  fluid inside the tubes. On the primary 
side, the reactor coolant flows downward across the outside of the tubes, transferring 
heat to the fluid inside the SG tubes. On  the secondary side, preheated feedwater 
enters the two feed plenums at the bottom  of each SG, flows up the helical tubes 
where it is heated, boiled, and superheated, and exits the two steam plenums at the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report Summary Description
Tier 2 5.1-5 Revision 5top of each SG. The DHRS is connected to the stea m and feed piping to permit use of 
the SG to remove decay heat  from the primary coolant.
The SGs are further described in Section 5.4.
5.1.4 System Evaluation
To optimize performance of the NuScale Power Plant over the range of  power levels within 
the design basis, steady state values for primary and secondary side parameters are 
established as a function of reactor power. Th e following criteria are considered to define 
optimal performance: maximizing electrical ge neration, using support systems efficiently, 
and providing margin to analytical limits . The following parameters are determined: 
primary coolant temperatures, primary coolant flow rates, PZR water level, SG water level 
and mass, feedwater and steam flow rates,  feedwater and steam pressure, and steam 
superheat. The steady state parameters are ca lculated using the thermal hydraulic software 
NRELAP5 and are verified by hand calculation.
Calculations establish a best-estimate flow , maximum flow, and minimum flow for the 
applicable design considerations , as well as the primary cool ant temperatures at each flow 
condition. In establishing the range of design flows, the calculations account for uncertainties in the component flow resistances, the amount of core bypass flow, and the 
thermal head capability. Bounding uncertaintie s are determined based on testing data and 
design requirements.
The pressure losses through the RCS flow pa th are very low, to support the natural 
circulation design of the RCS. Since the pressure losses due to flow are small, the pressure at any location in the RCS flow  path is primarily a function of the static head. The primary 
coolant flows over the outside of the helica l SG tubes; therefore,  SG tube plugging 
uncertainties are not applicable to the prim ary coolant natural circulation flow rate 
determination.
Minimum design flow is the lowest expected va lue for the primary coolant flow rate and is 
calculated by biasing analytical uncertaintie s to minimize the flow rate. Minimum design 
primary coolant flow rate is us ed in design analyses where it  is bounding to assume a low 
flow rate.
Maximum design flow is the highest expected value for the primary coolant flow rate. 
Maximum design flow is calculated by biasin g analytical uncertainties to maximize the 
primary coolant flow rate. Maximum design prim ary coolant flow rate is used in design 
analyses where it is bounding to assume a high flow rate.
The module heatup system is used to heat up the RCS and provide primary coolant flow 
before the reactor is critical . Between hot zero power and 15 percent reactor power, the 
heat generated from the reactor core is used to heat up the RCS to the normal operating 
average coolant temperature. Between 15 perc ent reactor power and full power operating 
conditions, the average primary coolant temperat ure is maintained at a constant value. The 
full power average coolant temperature is higher for higher pr imary coolant flow 
conditions, and lower for lo wer primary coolant flow co nditions. This phenomenon 
provides for similar hot leg te mperatures at full power condit ions, which allows for the SG 
to generate sufficient superheat independent of the full power primary coolant flow rate.
nuscale_ch05_0005.summary: This section describes the main components of the NuScale reactor system, focusing on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV), Reactor Coolant System (RCS) piping, and the Pressurizer (PZR). The RPV serves as a primary barrier to fission product release and houses key reactor internals, while the RCS piping connects various subsystems, and the PZR is integrated into the RPV to help control system pressure. Each component’s function and connections within the overall system are briefly outlined.

edges.description: Section A describes the general arrangement and references figures showing plant layout, while Section B explains the purpose of piping and instrumentation diagrams (P&IDs) that visually clarify system design, so both sections provide structural and visual context for understanding the arrangement of major components relevant to natural circulation analysis.

Section A provides the general arrangement and layout of major structures, including the vertical RPV, while Section B describes the natural circulation cooling system that relies on this arrangement, connecting the physical configuration to the system's ability to establish natural circulation.

Section A provides the physical arrangement of major structures and equipment, which includes the vertical RPV layout depicted in Figure 1.2-5, while Section B describes the operating characteristic that the NPM relies on natural circulation for reactor coolant flow, thus connecting the physical arrangement to the functional requirement for natural circulation.

Section B states that the NPM is designed to use natural circulation for reactor coolant flow, while Section A describes the system features (such as decay heat removal and passive condensers) that support and enable this natural circulation, thus providing evidence that the vertical RPV arrangement is adequate.

Section A describes the operational features and passive safety systems that rely on natural circulation, while Section B outlines the design basis of the RCS, explicitly stating that the system is designed to use natural circulation without pumps, thus Section A's operational details support and are consistent with Section B's design intent.
Section B provides detailed diagrams that visually clarify the system arrangements described in Section A, allowing verification of whether the vertical RPV arrangement supports natural circulation.

Section A states that the NPM is designed to use natural circulation for reactor coolant flow, while Section B describes the RCS design as relying on natural circulation without pumps, thus Section A's operational intent is satisfied by Section B's design basis.

Section A describes the design reliance on natural circulation within the RPV, while Section B provides the diagrams (including Figure 5.1-2) that visually clarify the arrangement and flow paths supporting this design basis.

Section B describes the closed-loop, two-phase natural circulation cooling system and its components, which relies on the vertical RPV arrangement shown in Figure 1.2-5 and the RCS diagram in Figure 5.1-2 referenced in the analysis question, thus providing operational details that support the adequacy of the arrangement discussed in Section A.

Section A describes the power conversion systems and their arrangement, while Section B introduces the general arrangement of major structures and equipment, with both sections referencing plant layout figures that provide context for evaluating the adequacy of the vertical RPV arrangement for natural circulation.

Section B describes the closed-loop, two-phase natural circulation cooling system and its reliance on system configuration, which is relevant to evaluating whether the vertical RPV arrangement shown in Section A's referenced figures is adequate for establishing natural circulation.

Section A discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure reactor vessel integrity under various conditions, while Section B describes the general arrangement of major structures and equipment, including figures that provide spatial context for system layouts; both sections are related through their reliance on plant configuration (as depicted in figures) to inform operational safety and procedural adequacy.

Section B describes the closed-loop, two-phase natural circulation cooling system enabled by the vertical RPV arrangement depicted in the referenced figures, while Section A focuses on operating procedures to maintain vessel integrity, thus Section B provides system-level context that supports the adequacy of the arrangement discussed in the analysis question.

Section A discusses the structural and examination requirements for RPV attachments, while Section B addresses the development of operating procedures to ensure pressure-temperature limits are not exceeded, both of which are connected through their focus on maintaining reactor vessel integrity under operational conditions.

Section B describes the closed-loop, two-phase natural circulation cooling system enabled by the vertical RPV arrangement depicted in Section A's referenced figures, thus providing evidence that supports the adequacy of the arrangement for natural circulation.

Section A describes specific features of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) relevant to natural circulation, while Section B references the general arrangement and layout figures that provide spatial context for these features, connecting the system's design to its physical configuration.

Section A describes the vertical RPV arrangement and its features for gas removal and pressure boundary integrity, while Section B explains that the RCS operates as a closed-loop, two-phase natural circulation system with passive decay heat removal, thus Section A's design details support the adequacy of natural circulation described in Section B.

Section B provides specific details about the reactor coolant system (RCS) configuration, including the vertical arrangement of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which can be visually corroborated by the cutaway and diagram referenced in Section A, thereby supporting the analysis of natural circulation adequacy.

Section A explains that certain SRP guidance is not applicable to the NuScale design because it lacks reactor coolant pumps and relies on natural circulation, while Section B provides the general arrangement of major structures and equipment, which includes the vertical RPV configuration relevant for assessing natural circulation adequacy.

Section A explains that NuScale's design does not use reactor coolant pumps and relies on natural circulation, while Section B describes the system's closed-loop, two-phase natural circulation cooling arrangement, thus Section B provides specific design details that clarify and support the applicability statement in Section A.

Section A discusses testing programs for unique design features supporting safety analysis, while Section B provides the general arrangement of major structures and equipment, so Section A's test data could support claims about the adequacy of the arrangement described in Section B.

Section A discusses testing programs that generate data to support safety analyses of design features, while Section B describes the RCS's natural circulation cooling system, meaning the test data from A is used to validate the adequacy of the natural circulation arrangement described in B.

Section A discusses the thermal hydraulic stability review and reactor trip methodology to prevent SAFDL violations, while Section B provides the general arrangement of major structures and equipment, including the RPV's vertical orientation, which is relevant for assessing natural circulation adequacy as required by Section A's safety criteria.

Section B describes the closed-loop, two-phase natural circulation cooling system and its components, which provides the design basis for evaluating whether the vertical RPV arrangement (referenced in the analysis question) can establish natural circulation, while Section A outlines the regulatory requirements and review responsibilities for ensuring thermal hydraulic stability and preventing SAFDL violations, thus Section B's system design is evaluated against Section A's regulatory criteria.

Section A describes operational procedures and system components relevant to establishing natural circulation in the RPV, while Section B provides the general physical arrangement of major structures and equipment, which includes the vertical RPV configuration referenced in the analysis question.

Section A describes the operational steps involving the chemical volume control system and pressurizer water level adjustments necessary to prepare the RPV for natural circulation, while Section B outlines the design and function of the closed-loop natural circulation cooling system, connecting the operational prerequisites in A to the system capabilities in B.

Section A explains that certain regulatory guidance for forced-flow PWRs is not applicable to the NuScale design due to its reliance on natural circulation, while Section B describes testing programs (such as critical heat flux testing) that provide data supporting the safety analysis of unique design features like natural circulation in the NuScale reactor.

Section A explains that the NuScale design does not use reactor coolant pumps and thus does not rely on forced circulation, while Section B clarifies that the design uses reactor trip signals to prevent thermal hydraulic instability, both highlighting the reliance on natural circulation as a fundamental design feature.

Section A explains that the NuScale design does not use reactor coolant pumps and thus relies on natural circulation, while Section B describes system components and operational steps (such as isolation and venting) that facilitate natural circulation in the vertical RPV arrangement, connecting the design approach to the operational adequacy for natural circulation.

Section A describes testing programs that generate data to support safety analyses, which in turn provide evidence that supports the regulatory conformance claims made in Section B regarding thermal hydraulic stability and protection against CHF violations.

Section A discusses testing programs that provide data to support safety analyses of unique design features, while Section B describes system components and operational procedures relevant to the reactor coolant system, with both sections providing information necessary to evaluate whether the vertical RPV arrangement supports natural circulation as required by the analysis question.

Section B describes the system components and operational steps (including the vertical RPV arrangement) that enable natural circulation, while Section A outlines the regulatory requirement to detect and suppress thermal hydraulic instabilities, connecting the adequacy of the arrangement in B to the satisfaction of the requirement in A.
